Manasseh became king at twelve years old and ruled for fifty-five years—and the entire span of his reign is marked by a systematic dismantling of everything his father Hezekiah had restored. Where Hezekiah had torn down the high places and called the nation back to the Lord, Manasseh rebuilt them. Where Hezekiah had cleansed the temple, Manasseh filled it with idols, even planting an Asherah pole in the very sanctuary where the Lord had promised to establish His Name forever. He did not merely tolerate idolatry; he led Judah into it with intention and power, corrupting the nation so thoroughly that they surpassed the very peoples God had driven out before them. And beneath all this religious betrayal ran a dark current of violence—Manasseh shed innocent blood until Jerusalem was filled with it. This is the brutal reality of a king who rejects God: not just personal failure, but the weaponization of royal authority against his own people.

The word of the Lord came through His servants the prophets, and it was a word of judgment without ambiguity. God would stretch over Jerusalem the same measuring line He had used against Samaria, the measuring line of destruction. The covenant promise—spoken to David and Solomon that the Lord would establish His Name in Jerusalem forever if the people kept His Law—becomes the very standard by which Jerusalem will be judged and found wanting. The people have not simply wandered from the covenant; they have been led astray by their king into greater evil than the nations before them. God will forsake the remnant of His inheritance and deliver them to their enemies. This is not the judgment of a distant deity but of the God who brought Israel out of Egypt, who loved them with an everlasting love, and who now confronts them with the weight of their choice to abandon Him.

Yet the chapter does not end with Manasseh's death and burial. His son Amon follows in his footsteps, walking in all his ways, serving and worshiping the same idols, abandoning the God of his fathers. But servants conspire against Amon and kill him in his palace—and then the people of the land rise up and strike down those who killed the king, placing Josiah on the throne. There is no explanation given for why the people act, no grand revelation of their hearts. But in the darkness of these successive reigns, in the murder and counter-murder, something turns. The people themselves step in, and a child named Josiah—whose name means "the Lord has healed"—becomes king. The text offers no promise, no prophecy, no assurance of what is to come. But the reader who knows the rest of the story knows that God has not abandoned His people, even when they have abandoned Him. Judgment is real, but so is the God who judges, and His purposes move forward even through the failures of kings and the violence of men.
